title: This thread is not about the taglines
display_title: This thread is not about the taglines
wikitext:
[]: This thread is (not) about the taglines             Quote Post by qu4d » Wed Jan 04, 2012 4:19 am Well... yeah. Now after the tagline of 3.0 is known and we are discussing it, I'd like to know what you think about the (not) thingy. Because I guess the film will leave the question unanswered if you can redo things or not. Or do you think otherswise? Are you alone or not? Can you advance or not? I don't think that the movies were clear about that...

[]:             Quote Post by Monk Ed » Wed Jan 04, 2012 7:10 am "You are (not) alone" was an excellent tagline full of a lot of meaning for me. However, the continued usage of it beyond that first time severely diminished its impact. Which is not to say I hate it or anything. I just think none but the first got the full value out of its usage. It also helps that I feel like "You are (not) alone" is a pretty good descriptor of human existence period, and feels like the sum-total lesson of the whole NGE series.

[]:             Quote Post by Axell » Wed Jan 04, 2012 12:32 pm In one of the translations for 1.11, Misato even uses the line "you are not alone" to Shinji, which i felt was very nice. The official subs didnt quite phrase it as such and i was a little disappointed about that. 2.22 didnt seem to use the line anywhere, and I cant think of a place where it would fit into the dialogue anyway. I guess in the end its another one of those marketing things, the english title sounds cool or exciting to someone in Japan but for native speakers the lack of substance behind it it more noticeable. *disappears again*

[]:             Quote Post by Fireand'chutes77 » Wed Jan 04, 2012 1:02 pm Monk Ed wrote: "You are (not) alone" was an excellent tagline full of a lot of meaning for me. However, the continued usage of it beyond that first time severely diminished its impact. Which is not to say I hate it or anything. I just think none but the first got the full value out of its usage. It also helps that I feel like "You are (not) alone" is a pretty good descriptor of human existence period, and feels like the sum-total lesson of the whole NGE series.

[]:             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Wed Jan 04, 2012 1:23 pm I agree with Monk Ed that You Are (Not) Alone has the most powerful impact so far, and how nice it is that one of the translations included it. Would be nice if the other films included their international titles somehow, but how they'd fit...Yeah I dunno.

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Wed Jan 04, 2012 3:01 pm I guess I agree that You Are (Not) Alone had the most impact, but I'll also go a bit further and say that the reason why it had a large impact is because it is the first realization people universally make. People naturally tend to look toward themselves first when taking on tasks or sifting through hardships. When this realization strikes them, it's a major game-changer. It means the difference between happiness or despair, accomplishment or failure. To see Shinji make this realization in You are (Not) Alone was really touching for me. The shot where he finally squeezes back the hand of Misato really moved be, and encapsulated everything the movie was about. You Can (Not) Advance is just the natural progression of things. Once one realizes that s/he isn't alone, that person can advance to the next stage. It seems quite natural to advance after building confidence in others, so it's impact is diminished simply because the concept itself isn't quite the game-changer. But this newer title takes on the opposite form the previous one. In the title You Are (Not) Alone, the parenthetical is very assuring to the viewer. Without it, it would read "You Are Alone", obviously causing emotions of anxiety in the viewer. But the parenthetical doesn't always play the part of the reassure in these titles, and that's one of the reasons why the title You Can (Not) Advance still holds some strength in my mind. (Still not as strong as "You are (Not) Alone", but still.) In a part of the title where the viewer was once able to find reassurance, it is suddenly taken away. It reads "You Can Advance" when the parenthetical is removed, which would have been just as reassuring as the added parenthetical in You are (Not) Alone. This also makes the viewer question the validity of these parentheses. Can they be trusted? Was the "(Not)" in "You are (Not) Alone" as true as the "(Not)" in "You Can (Not) Advance"? Are neither of them true? Is Shinji truly alone, but still have the ability to advance despite his solitude? Or is the collective group that found reassurance in others going to ultimately fail in their attempted advancements? Can a parenthetical be true in one statement, but false in the other? Where as the entirety of "You Are (Not) Alone" is the game-changer, the entirety of "You Can (Not) Advance" could prove to be the unsuspecting foil. the titles can almost be construed as cruel to the viewers in this regard. You Can (Not) Redo would, with the absence of the parenthetical, suggest ultimate power. After all, if one could go back and change things in that person's past, then that person would have enough power to have everything go in that person's favor. This is when the validity (or the probable lack thereof) of these parentheses really needs to be brought into focus, possibly changing everything the viewer once thought about these titles. If the "(Not)" in "You Can (Not) Advance" be canceled out by the teamwork of those who've Shinji had confided in "You Are (Not) Alone", then can the "(Not)" in "You Can (Not) Redo" be just as systematically canceled out? And in the end, this all needs to boil down to who these titles are referring to. You Are (Not) Alone : Shinji finds some form of companionship with Misato and Rei, as well as Asuka and the beginnings of the realization of Yui in the following film. It all encompasses to these four women, who eventually in some way become either a help or a foil to Shinji's relationship with Rei specifically. You Can (Not) Advance : More Angels are defeated, giving the appearance of advancement, but to what end? What was the cause the characters were supposed to be advancing toward? And, can it really be called an "advancement"? Also, can Shinji really advance in the relationships with the four core women, particularly with Rei? Or will he be brought back to the state of loneliness he found himself in the beginning of first film by losing Rei? (This would bring the validity of the parenthetical in "You Are (Not) Alone" into a possibly frightening new light.) It all boils down to Third Impact and how Shinji's human relationships effect it, though nothing is answered in the end. You Can (Not) Redo : Just as each film threatens the validity of the previous films' title ("You Can (Not) Advance" dangerously threatens the lives of those within Shinji has found comfort, as suggested by the "You Are (Not) Alone" title) "You Can (Not) Redo" by its very nature threatens everything suggested within the previous films' titles. If everything can (or will) be redone, will Shinji be alone in the end? Will he be able to advance again if he ever did at all in the first place? Or does it simply not matter because the title in its entirety suggests that nothing can (or will) be redone? Or will the questions still go unanswered until... "FINAL"? : Whether or not you still have questions, the series ends here.

[]:             Quote Post by Ironfoot » Wed Jan 04, 2012 6:12 pm Pretty much what FFF4E stated, also the fact that it'd be odd to start a new naming mechanism at this point. The pattern is already there. You can (not) Redo reminds me of Anno's past failures and it makes me wonder if he'll come to terms with his mistakes.

[]:             Quote Post by the_seventh_child » Wed Jan 04, 2012 7:46 pm First time I saw the title, I thought it was kind of brilliant; one line that can set the mood for a whole franchise. I guess, like Monk Ed said, the continued usage of this technique would inevitably kind of 'wear out' rest of the titles in the audience's mind, (with the exception of the first one, that really stands out being the pioneer and all) but it's still something really neat. The flawless post of FFF4E captures the rest of what I would be typing.

[]:             Quote Post by robersora » Thu Jan 05, 2012 2:54 am Yep FFF4E's post was great, but I'd like to add one thing. Some of you complained, that while "you are (not) alone" is somehow used in the first movie, "you can (not) advance isn't. This is not true. For instance, after the activation of EVA03 goes wrong, Asuka is not here anymore, also Shinji leaves Nerv. Maya then says something like "We are back to the beginning". For me this is the justification for the title at least plot wise. But you could even use it for the end of the title. 10 Angels might have been defeated, but Tokyo 3 is in ashes, Rei AND Shinji are trapped in EVA01, and Asuka is still hors de combat. Agree, disagree? Well, I hope, we get an explanation, what Anno really was thinking. I hope it was so deep how FFF4E said and not only "It sounded cool, LOLZ" But I think, he used English here, because, as far I now, it is not possible to negate a sentence in such an easy manner in Japanese... Well, I was astonished by "you can (not) redo" at first, foremost, because I expected an other auxiliary verb like "must". I think a title with a "must" in it would have been very interesting, because for me as a non-native speaker, there is a greater difference between "must" and "must-not" than in say "can" and "cannot". But, well... Another thing I might to add, why do you think they changed the Kanji 急 to Q for the third film? Maybe because it isn't the ending? Only because of fanciness? Or did they think of something else? Ok, they could use the Q as Quickening (but is this still prevalent?). And what could Quickening mean? I read somewhere, the movements of Babies in the uterus are called this way, well then it might imply that we get some past for the characters of NERV? What do you all think? :)

[]:             Quote Post by symbv » Thu Jan 05, 2012 4:19 am robersora wrote: But I think, he used English here, because, as far I now, it is not possible to negate a sentence in such an easy manner in Japanese...

[]:             Quote Post by robersora » Thu Jan 05, 2012 6:52 am symbv wrote: Mind a bit of elaboration here? I can easily negate "You are alone" in Japanese for example: 君、ひとりぼっち（じゃない）

[]:             Quote Post by symbv » Thu Jan 05, 2012 9:19 am robersora wrote: But can you do that also with "you can (not) advance" and "you can (not) redo" in a similar way?

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Thu Jan 05, 2012 9:24 am @ robersora: When it comes to reciting the title of the movie in the movies itself, I've found it more laughable than not most of the time. Or at least not all that powerful. (It didn't hold any power in the dialogue of any of the Lord of the Rings movies.) But the statement "You are not alone" is a srong statement in the context of the film, even though the line itself is Japanese and not the English title. It really added weight to the film. The line "We are back at the beginning" or whatever in the second film I usually see as a more clever and natural way of bringing the film's dialogue back to the subject of the film's title. As for the word "must" as opposed to "can", there are two other words that could substitute, but I don't think work that well. One is "may". (You May (Not) Redo.) This suggests permission rather than ability, and doesn't quite work as well expressing the film's possible point. The other is the one you suggested, "must". (You Must (Not) Redo.) This word has more to do with a pressing need to flex one's ability to do something. Whereas this idea might be cool when it comes to playing with the goals Shinji needs to address in the film, it doesn't really do well to make the main character feel as helpless as the word "can" would. "Can" has to do with one's ability to do something to begin with. For me, this harkens back to some of the ideas in Ep. 26 of EoTV, when Shinji has physical boundries and restrictions established for him. ("I'll give you a restriction." "There, now you have a heaven and earth." "But now, you've lost one aspect of your freedom." "You must stand on the ground." "But you feel more secure.") I feel as if the use of the word "can" is playing with that sense of physical restriction. "You Can (Not) Advance/Redo" doesn't let on to the idea of giving the character the ability to do anything in the first place, much less let him accomplish what he "must" do.

[]:             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Thu Jan 05, 2012 11:03 am symbv wrote: I think at first they planned only 3 movies and thus used the movement structure in traditional Japanese arts, Jo Ha Kyuu, to name them. But later the plan got into 4 movies

[]:             Quote Post by symbv » Thu Jan 05, 2012 12:13 pm ^ That youtube video does not have 序破急 in it. Interested in seeing one with 序破急 in it. But then even if there is one showing 序破急 with 1.0, 2.0, 3.0 and Final, it only proves that as early as 2007 they had 4 films in mind. What I was thinking is in an even earlier stage, they might have thought about 3 films and put 序破急 to it. Later they added one film but kept the naming convention 序破急 because they still liked the idea of using 序破急 (and indeed they used 序 and 破 for the actual movies) but without a good idea of what to call the Final in kanji, used a ? instead.

[]:             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Thu Jan 05, 2012 12:46 pm symbv wrote: Interested in seeing one with 序破急 in it.

[]:             Quote Post by symbv » Thu Jan 05, 2012 12:50 pm ^ Actually I was talking about the planning stage before any news was broken to the media. Granted it is purely speculation on my part.

[]:             Quote Post by JoeD80 » Thu Jan 05, 2012 1:41 pm This one has 序破急? http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZDoTPdNAtBU

[]:             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Thu Jan 05, 2012 1:54 pm JoeD80 wrote: This one has 序破急?

[]:             Quote Post by symbv » Thu Jan 05, 2012 2:14 pm Cool !! Not really relevant to what I was speculating, but nice to see the trailer anyway.
